volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Rope (Section 2.6.531 - Section 3.2.635)

sections.label: Section 2.6.531
sections.original: Vt fortunati sunt fabri ferrarii ,
sections.translation: How lucky are the blacksmiths who are always sitting among hot coals ; they are always warm .

sections.label: Section 2.6.533
sections.original: Vtinam fortuna nunc anetina úterer ,
sections.translation: I only wish that I were now enjoying the lot of the duck , so as , although I had just come from out of the water , still to be dry .

sections.label: Section 2.6.535
sections.original: Quid si aliquo ad ludos me pro manduco locem ?
sections.translation: What if I some way or other let myself out at the games for a hobgoblin [*] ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.536
sections.original: Quapropter ?
sections.translation: For what reason ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.536b
sections.original: Quia pol clare crepito dentibus .
sections.translation: Because , I ’ faith , I ’ m chattering aloud with my teeth .

sections.label: Section 2.6.537
sections.original: Iure optumo me élavisse árbitror .
sections.translation: But I ’ m of opinion that , with very good reason , I ’ ve had this ducking .

sections.label: Section 2.6.538
sections.original: Qui ?
sections.translation: How so ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.538b
sections.original: Qui un a auderem tecum in navem ascendere ,
sections.translation: Why , haven ’ t I ventured to go on board ship with yourself , who have been stirring up the ocean for me from the very bottom ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.540
sections.original: Tibi auscultavi , tu promittebas mihi
sections.translation: I listened to you when advising me ; you assured me that there in Sicily was very great profit from courtesans ; there , you used to say , I should be able to amass wealth .

sections.label: Section 2.6.543
sections.original: Iam postulabas te , impurata belua ,
sections.translation: Did you expect , then , you unclean beast , that you were going to gobble up the whole island of Sicily ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.545
sections.original: Quaenam ballaena meum voravit vidulum ,
sections.translation: What whale , I wonder , has gobbled up my wallet where all my gold and silver was packed up ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.547
sections.original: Eadem illa , credo , quae meum marsuppium ,
sections.translation: That same one , I suppose , that has swallowed my purse , which was full of silver in my travelling-bag .

sections.label: Section 2.6.549
sections.original: Eheu , redactus sum usque ad unam hanc tuniculam
sections.translation: Alas ! I ’ m reduced even to this one poor tunic ( stretching it out )

sections.label: Section 2.6.550
sections.original: et ad hoc misellum pallium . perii oppido .
sections.translation: and to this poor shabby cloak ; I ’ m done for to all intents .

sections.label: Section 2.6.551
sections.original: Vel consociare mihi quidem tecum licet :
sections.translation: Then you may even go into partnership with me ; we have got equal shares .

sections.label: Section 2.6.552b
sections.original: Saltem si mihi
sections.translation: If at least my damsels had been saved , there would have been some hope . Now , if the young man Plesidippus should be seeing me ,

sections.label: Section 2.6.555
sections.original: quo ab arrabonem pro Palaestra acceperam ,
sections.translation: from whom I received the earnest for Palaestra , he ’ ll then be causing me some trouble in consequence .

sections.label: Section 2.6.557
sections.original: Quid , stulte , ploras ? tibi quidem edepol copiast ,
sections.translation: Why cry , you fool ? Really , by my troth , so long as your tongue shall exist , you have abundance with which to make payment to everybody [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.7.559
sections.original: Quíd illuc , opsecro , negotist quod duae mulierculae
sections.translation: What to-do is this , I ’ d like to know , that two young women

sections.label: Section 2.7.560
sections.original: hic in fano Veneris signum flentes amplexae tenent ,
sections.translation: here in the Temple , in tears , are holding in their embrace the statue of Venus , dreading I know not what in their wretchedness ? But they say that this last night they have been tossed about , and to-day cast on shore from the waves .

sections.label: Section 2.7.563
sections.original: Opsecro hercle , adulescens , ubi istaec sunt quas memoras mulieres ?
sections.translation: Troth now , young man , prithee , where are these young women that you are talking of ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.564
sections.original: Hic in fano Veneris .
sections.translation: Here ( pointing ) in the Temple of Venus .

sections.label: Section 2.7.564b
sections.original: Quot sunt ?
sections.translation: How many are there ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.564c
sections.original: Totidem quot ego et tu sumus .
sections.translation: Just as many as you and I make .

sections.label: Section 2.7.565
sections.original: Nempe meae ?
sections.translation: Surely , they are mine .

sections.label: Section 2.7.565b
sections.original: Nempe néscio istuc .
sections.translation: Surely , I know nothing about that .

sections.label: Section 2.7.565c
sections.original: Qua sunt facie ?
sections.translation: Of what appearance are they ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.565d
sections.original: Scitula .
sections.translation: Good-looking ; I could even fall in love with either of them , if I were well liquored .

sections.label: Section 2.7.567
sections.original: Nempe puellae ?
sections.translation: Surely , they are the damsels .

sections.label: Section 2.7.567b
sections.original: Nempe moléstus es . i vise , si lubet .
sections.translation: Surely , you are a nuisance ; be off , go in and see , if you like .

sections.label: Section 2.7.568
sections.original: Meas oportet intus esse hic mulieres , mi Charmides .
sections.translation: These must be my wenches in here , my dear Charmides .

sections.label: Section 2.7.569
sections.original: Iuppiter te perdat , et si sunt , et si non sunt tamen .
sections.translation: Jupiter confound you , both if they are and still if they are not .

sections.label: Section 2.7.570
sections.original: Intro rumpam iam huc in Veneris fanum .—
sections.translation: I ’ ll straightway burst into this Temple of Venus here .

sections.label: Section 2.7.570b
sections.original: In barathrum mavelim .
sections.translation: Into the bottomless pit , I would rather . ( LABRAX rushes into the Temple , and shuts the door .) Prithee , stranger , show me some spot where I may go to sleep .

sections.label: Section 2.7.572
sections.original: Istic ubi vis condormisce ; nemo prohibet , publicum est .
sections.translation: Go to sleep there , wherever you please ( points to the ground ) ; no one hinders , it ’ s free to the public .

sections.label: Section 2.7.573
sections.original: At vides me ornatus ut sim vestimentis uvidis :
sections.translation: But do you see me , in what wet clothes I ’ m dressed ? Do take me under shelter ; lend me some dry clothes ,

sections.label: Section 2.7.575
sections.original: dum arescunt mea ; ín aliquo tibi gratiam referam loco .
sections.translation: while my own are drying ; on some occasion I ’ ll return you the favour .

sections.label: Section 2.7.576
sections.original: Tegillum eccillud , mihi unum id aret ; id si vis , dabo :
sections.translation: See , here ’ s my outer coat , which alone is dry ; that , if you like , I ’ ll lend you . ( Takes it off and holds it out to him .) In that same I ’ m wont to be clothed , by that same protected , when it rains . Do you give me those clothes of yours ; I ’ ll soon have them dried .

sections.label: Section 2.7.578b
sections.original: Eho an te paenitet ,
sections.translation: How now , are you afraid that , as I ’ ve been washed bare [*] last night at sea , I mayn ’ t be made bare again here upon shore ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.580
sections.original: Eluas tu an exunguare , ciccum non interduim .
sections.translation: Wash you bare , or anoint you well , I don ’ t care one fig [*] . I shall never entrust anything to you unless upon a pledge being taken . Do you either sweat away or perish with cold , be you either sick or well . I ’ ll put up with no stranger-guest in my house ; I ’ ve had disagreements enough .

sections.label: Section 2.7.584
sections.original: Iamne abis ? venalis illic ductitavit , quisquis est ;
sections.translation: What , are you off ? ( A pause .) He ’ s a trafficker in slaves for money [*] ; whoever he is ,

sections.label: Section 2.7.585
sections.original: non est misericors . sed quid ego hic asto infelix uvidus ?
sections.translation: he has no bowels [*] of compassion . But why in my wretchedness am I standing here , soaking ? Why don ’ t I rather go away from here into the Temple of Venus , that I may sleep off this debauch which I got with drinking last night against the bent of my inclination ? Neptune has been drenching us with salt water as though we were Greek wines [*] , and so he hoped that our stomachs might be vomited up with his salt draughts .

sections.label: Section 2.7.590
sections.original: quid opust verbis ? si invitare nos paulisper pergeret ,
sections.translation: What need of words ? If he had persisted in inviting us a little longer , we should have gone fast asleep there ; as it is , hardly alive has he sent us off home . Now I ’ ll go see the Procurer , my boon companion , what he ’ s doing within .

sections.label: Section 3.1.593
sections.original: Miris modis di ludos faciunt hominibus :
sections.translation: In wondrous ways [*] do the Gods make sport of men , in wondrous fashions do they send dreams in sleep .

sections.label: Section 3.1.595
sections.original: ne dormientis quidem sinunt quiescere .
sections.translation: Not the sleeping , even , do they allow to rest . As , for example , I , this last night which has gone by , dreamed a wonderful and a curious dream . A she-ape seemed to be endeavouring to climb up to a swallow ’ s nest ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.600
sections.original: neque eas eripere quibat inde . postibi
sections.translation: and she was not able thence to take them out . After that , the ape seemed to come to me to beg me to lend a ladder to her . I in these terms gave answer to the ape , that swallows are the descendants of Philomela [*] and of Progne .

sections.label: Section 3.1.605
sections.original: ago cum ílla , ne quid noceat meis popularibus .
sections.translation: I expostulated with her , that she might not hurt those of my country . But then she began to be much more violent , and seemed gratuitously to be threatening me with vengeance . She summoned me to a court of justice . Then , in my anger , I seemed to seize hold of the ape by the middle , in what fashion I know not ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.610
sections.original: concludo in vincla bestiam nequissimam .
sections.translation: and I fastened up with chains this most worthless beast . Now to what purpose I shall say that this dream tends , never have I this day been able to come to any conclusion . ( A loud noise is heard in the Temple .) But what ’ s this noise that arises in this Temple of Venus , my neighbour ? My mind ’ s in wonder about it .

sections.label: Section 3.2.615
sections.original: Pró Cyrenenses populares , vostram ego imploro fidem ,
sections.translation: O citizens of Cyrene , I implore your aid , countrymen , you who are near neighbours to these spots , bring aid to helplessness , and utterly crush a most vile attempt . Inflict vengeance , that the power of the wicked , who wish themselves to be distinguished by crimes , may not be stronger than of the guiltless .

sections.label: Section 3.2.620
sections.original: statuite exemplum impudenti , date pudori praemium ,
sections.translation: Make an example for the shameless man , give its reward to modest virtue ; cause that one may be allowed to live here rather under the control of the laws than of brute force . Hasten hither into the Temple of Venus ; again do I implore your aid , you who are here at hand and who hear my cries .

sections.label: Section 3.2.624
sections.original: ferte suppetias qui Veneri Veneriaeque antistitae
sections.translation: Bring assistance to those who , after the recognized usage , have entrusted their lives to Venus and to the Priestess of Venus , under their protection .

sections.label: Section 3.2.626
sections.original: praetorquete iniuriae prius cóllum quam ad vos pervenat .
sections.translation: Wring ye the neck of iniquity before it reaches yourselves .

sections.label: Section 3.2.627
sections.original: Quid istuc est negoti ?
sections.translation: What ’ s all this to-do ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.627b
sections.original: Per ego haec genua te optestor , senex ,
sections.translation: By these knees of yours , I do entreat you , old gentleman , whoever you are —

sections.label: Section 3.2.628b
sections.original: Quin tu ergo omitte genua et quid sit mi expedi
sections.translation: Nay , but do you let go my knees , then , and tell me why it is that you are making a noise ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.629b
sections.original: Teque oro et quaeso , si speras tibi
sections.translation: I do beg and entreat you , that if you hope

sections.label: Section 3.2.630
sections.original: hoc anno multum futurum sirpe et laserpicium
sections.translation: this year that you will have abundance of laserwort and silphium [*] , and that that export will arrive at Capua [*] safe and sound , and that you may ever enjoy freedom from diseased eyes —

sections.label: Section 3.2.633
sections.original: Sanun es ?
sections.translation: Are you in your senses ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.633b
sections.original: Seu tibi confidis fore multam magudarim
sections.translation: — Or whether you trust that you will have plenty of juice of silphium [*] , that you will not hesitate to give me the aid which I shall entreat of you , aged sir .

sections.label: Section 3.2.635
sections.original: At ego te per crura et talos tergumque obtestor tuom ,
sections.translation: And I , by your legs , and ancles , and back , do entreat you that , if you hope that you will have a crop of elm-twigs , and that a fruitful harvest of beatings will this year be your lot , you will tell me what ’ s the matter here , by reason of which you are making this uproar .
